content:
Key Findings

  • The GOVN - Policy & Governance department at Chorley Borough Council spent £245.1M between 2021/22 and 2025/26.
  • This department accounted for 65.9% of the council's total £372.2M expenditure.
  • The second-largest spending department, also named Policy & Governance, spent £39.1M.
  • Customer & Digital services received £21.5M, representing 5.8% of total spending.
  • Commercial & Property services received £19.6M, accounting for 5.3% of total spending.

Policy & Governance Dominates Council Spending

The GOVN - Policy & Governance department at Chorley Borough Council is by far the largest spending department, with £245.1M allocated between 2021/22 and 2025/26. This substantial figure represents 65.9% of the council's total expenditure of £372.2M during this period. The sheer scale of this spending highlights the central role that policy and governance functions play in the council's operations.

Interestingly, there is another department named simply "Policy & Governance" which spent £39.1M, making it the second-largest spending department. This duplication in department naming could potentially cause confusion or indicate separate but related functions within the council's governance structure.

Breakdown of Departmental Spending

Beyond the dominant Policy & Governance departments, other areas of council activity received significant funding. The CUST - Customer & Digital department spent £21.5M, while GROW - Commercial & Property allocated £19.6M. These figures represent 5.8% and 5.3% of total spending, respectively, underscoring the council's investment in digital services and commercial property management.

The table below shows the top 10 spending departments, providing a clear picture of where the council's resources are directed. Notably, the PLAN - Planning & Development department, which received £5.9M, is the lowest-ranked department in the top 10, highlighting the relatively smaller scale of its budget compared to others.

Rank Department Amount % of Total
1 GOVN - Policy & Governance £245.1M 65.9%
2 Policy & Governance £39.1M 10.5%
3 CUST - Customer & Digital £21.5M 5.8%
4 GROW - Commercial & Property £19.6M 5.3%
5 GROW - Commercial Services £12.1M 3.2%
6 EARL - Communities £11.4M 3.1%
7 Commercial & Property £10.5M 2.8%
8 PLAN - Planning & Development £5.9M 1.6%
9 Customer & Digital £3.5M 0.9%
10 Communities £3.1M 0.8%

Understanding the Spending Distribution

The significant disparity between the top-spending department and the rest is notable. The £245.1M allocated to GOVN - Policy & Governance dwarfs the £39.1M spent by the similarly named Policy & Governance department. This suggests that the former may encompass a broader range of governance activities or have a more expansive remit within the council's operations.

The remaining departments, while receiving smaller amounts, still play crucial roles. For instance, the EARL - Communities department, which spent £11.4M, and the GROW - Commercial Services department, which allocated £12.1M, indicate investments in community services and commercial activities. These figures, though smaller, are vital for understanding the council's priorities and service delivery.

What You Can Do

  • Submit a Freedom of Information request to Chorley Borough Council about the specific activities funded by the GOVN - Policy & Governance department.
  • Attend council committee meetings to learn more about how policy and governance decisions are made and how funds are allocated.
  • Check the council's published contracts register to see detailed information about suppliers and contracts related to policy and governance.
  • Write to your local councillor to express any concerns or questions about the significant spending by the GOVN - Policy & Governance department.
  • Visit aidoge.co.uk to explore the full spending data and gain a deeper understanding of Chorley Borough Council's financial activities.
This article is based on Chorley Borough Council's published spending data covering 2021/22 to 2025/26. All figures are drawn directly from transactions published under the Local Government Transparency Code. Analysis by AI DOGE.
